name: Harold Chen
chief_complaint: My wife found me on the kitchen floor. I don't really remember what happened. She says I was shaking and sweating and talking nonsense.
onset_description: Patient's wife found him on the kitchen floor at approximately 6:30 AM, confused, diaphoretic, and tremulous. He had not eaten breakfast yet but had taken his morning insulin as usual. Wife gave him orange juice and called 911. EMS found blood glucose of 28 mg/dL and administered IV dextrose en route with improvement in mental status, though patient remains somewhat confused.

altered_mental_status.description: I'm a little foggy. I remember getting up and going to the kitchen, then the next thing I know the paramedics were there. My wife says I was talking gibberish.
altered_mental_status.onset: Approximately 1 hour ago, improving after dextrose
altered_mental_status.character: confusion, disorientation, brief unresponsiveness, now improving post-treatment

diaphoresis.description: I was drenched in sweat. My pajamas were soaked through. I'm still sweating a little.
diaphoresis.character: profuse cold sweating, adrenergic response to hypoglycemia

tremor.description: My hands are still shaking. My wife says my whole body was shaking when she found me.
tremor.character: fine tremor of extremities, adrenergic response

weakness.description: I feel wrung out, like I ran a marathon. My legs are wobbly.

headache.description: I have a dull headache now. Not terrible but it's there.

palpitations.description: My heart was racing. I can still feel it pounding.
palpitations.character: awareness of rapid heart rate, adrenergic response

labs.glucose_ED_arrival: 142 mg/dL (after IV dextrose and oral juice)
labs.BMP: Na 138, K 4.0, Cr 1.6 (elevated, baseline 1.4), BUN 28, Glucose 142
labs.liver_function: AST 28, ALT 32, Alk Phos 88, Albumin 3.4 - within normal limits
labs.troponin: 0.03 ng/mL (normal, obtained due to tachycardia and age)
labs.CT_head: No acute intracranial abnormality. Age-appropriate atrophy. No evidence of stroke.

past_medical_history: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (22 years, on insulin for 8 years)
Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline Cr 1.4, GFR 48)
Previous hypoglycemic episode requiring EMS (1 year ago)
medications: Insulin lispro 8 units before meals (fixed dose, does not carb count)
allergies: ACE inhibitor cough (tried lisinopril alternatives, tolerates current dose)

social_history.alcohol: Rare, a small glass of wine at family gatherings
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife of 48 years in a single-story home. Wife manages most of his medications and meals. Two adult children live locally and are involved in care.
social_history.exercise: Walks around the neighborhood daily for 20-30 minutes. Less active in winter.
social_history.diet: Wife prepares balanced meals, follows diabetic diet fairly well. Eats at regular times. Recently has had decreased appetite.

conditions.mother: Type 2 Diabetes, died at 78 from renal failure

personality: Quiet and reserved. Somewhat embarrassed about the incident. Defers to his wife for details. Meticulous about following doctor's orders but does not fully understand his medication regimen. Does not want to bother anyone.
communication_style: Soft-spoken with a slight accent. Gives concise, polite answers. His wife often supplements or corrects his responses. May say he understands when he doesn't. Nods a lot. Becomes visibly uncomfortable with too many questions at once. Prefers simple, clear explanations.
pain_description_style: Minimizes discomfort. Will say he feels 'okay' even when he clearly does not. Relies on his wife to report how he really looked. Rates pain low on the scale even when other signs suggest otherwise.

critical_findings: Severe hypoglycemia (glucose 28 mg/dL) with altered mental status requiring EMS intervention
Recurrent hypoglycemia (prior episode 1 year ago) indicating systemic management problem
Polypharmacy contributing to hypoglycemia risk: basal insulin + bolus insulin + glipizide + metformin is aggressive for a 72-year-old with CKD
CKD stage 3a reduces renal clearance of insulin and glipizide, prolonging hypoglycemic effect
Metoprolol masks adrenergic symptoms of hypoglycemia (tachycardia, tremor)
Peripheral neuropathy further reduces hypoglycemia awareness
Fixed-dose mealtime insulin without carb counting increases hypoglycemia risk with variable intake
Recently decreased appetite reported by wife — reduced food intake with unchanged insulin = hypoglycemia
Age and comorbidities make tight glycemic control (target HbA1c <7%) inappropriate — less stringent targets recommended
rubric.diagnosis: Severe hypoglycemia secondary to insulin and sulfonylurea use in the setting of CKD and decreased oral intake
differential_diagnoses: Transient ischemic attack or stroke (ruled out by CT and improving mental status with glucose)
Insulinoma (rare, but consider if recurrent unexplained hypoglycemia)
recommended_interventions: Continuous glucose monitoring in ED (check every 30-60 minutes for rebound hypoglycemia)
D10 infusion to maintain glucose > 100 mg/dL (sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia can recur for 24-72 hours)
HOLD glipizide — strongly recommend discontinuation given CKD and recurrent hypoglycemia
REDUCE insulin glargine dose (32 units likely too high given decreased appetite and renal function)
Reassess need for fixed-dose mealtime insulin vs. carb-counting approach
Consider relaxing HbA1c target to <8% given age, CKD, coronary disease, and hypoglycemia history
Observation for at least 12-24 hours (glipizide has long duration of action, especially in CKD)
CT head to rule out stroke (obtained, normal)
Educate patient AND wife about hypoglycemia recognition and treatment
Provide glucagon kit prescription for home use
Continuous glucose monitor (CGM) referral with hypoglycemia alerts
Assess and treat any underlying cause of decreased appetite
